STAT9005 – Time Series: Week 4 Study Guide

Classical Models – Lab Review, Decomposition & Exponential Smoothing

📝 Note to self: These notes combine the Week 4 lecture (Aula_4), the Lab 1 handout, the Lab 1 solution R script, and the Lecture 2 Classical Models slides. Gaps and follow-up slides are flagged throughout.

1 Key Topics

  • Lab 1 walkthrough: Kings, Births, and Souvenirs datasets
  • How to identify a valid time series vs. a non-time-series problem
  • Visual identification of time series components (trend, seasonality, residual)
  • Additive vs. multiplicative decomposition — choosing the right one
  • Residual analysis: confirming a good decomposition
  • STL decomposition (Seasonal & Trend decomposition using Loess)
  • Forecasting using decomposition (naïve + STL-based)
  • Introduction to Simple Exponential Smoothing (SES)
  • Overview of the full classical models family (SES → Holt → Holt-Winters)
  • Upcoming topics: Holt trend methods, Holt-Winters, error metrics, AIC/BIC

2 Core Explanations

2.1 Is It Actually a Time Series? — The Kings Dataset

Before doing any analysis, you must verify that your data actually qualifies as a time series. The first requirement is equally spaced observations — data points must be recorded at regular, consistent intervals.

The Kings dataset records the ages at death of successive Kings of England. At first glance it looks like sequential data, but it is not a time series. Each value is just the age a king happened to die — there is no regular time interval between observations. This is better framed as a regression problem (predicting the next king’s age at death), not a forecasting problem. Francisco made this distinction explicitly in class: we forget about the Kings dataset for this module.

The rule of thumb: ask yourself “Am I predicting future values of a process that unfolds over equally spaced time?” If yes, it is a time series. If no, it is something else.

2.2 Loading Data and the ts() Function

In R, raw data loaded with scan() is just a numeric vector — it has no time awareness. The ts() function wraps that vector with time series metadata.

# Kings — no frequency info needed (annual-ish, frequency = 1 by default)
kings <- scan("http://robjhyndman.com/tsdldata/misc/kings.dat", skip = 3)
kingstimeseries <- ts(kings)

# Births — monthly data starting January 1946
births <- scan("http://robjhyndman.com/tsdldata/data/nybirths.dat")
birthstimeseries <- ts(births, frequency = 12, start = c(1946, 1))

# Souvenir sales — monthly data starting January 1987
souvenir <- scan("http://robjhyndman.com/tsdldata/data/fancy.dat")
souvenirtimeseries <- ts(souvenir, frequency = 12, start = c(1987, 1))

The key arguments are:

  • frequency — number of observations per cycle (12 = monthly, 4 = quarterly, 1 = annual)
  • start — vector giving the start time, e.g. c(1946, 1) means January 1946

Always plot immediately after creating the time series object to get your first visual read on the data.

par(mfrow = c(1, 3))
plot.ts(kingstimeseries)
plot.ts(birthstimeseries)
plot.ts(souvenirtimeseries)

2.3 Identifying Components Visually

Every time series is assumed to be a function of up to four components:

\[y_t = f(S_t, T_t, R_t)\]

Where:

  • \(y_t\) = observed value at time \(t\)
  • \(S_t\) = seasonal component — repeating patterns within a calendar year
  • \(T_t\) = trend-cycle component — long-run direction (includes multi-year cycles)
  • \(R_t\) = remainder/residual — everything unexplained (random noise)

When you plot your data, you are trying to answer three questions visually:

1. Is there a trend?
Look for a consistent upward or downward direction over the full series. The births dataset shows a clear increasing linear trend, interrupted by a notable drop around 1955–1956.

2. What type of seasonality is present?
Compare the amplitude (height) of seasonal swings across different years: - If the swings stay roughly the same height → homogeneous seasonality → use additive model - If the swings grow bigger as the series increases → increasing/multiplicative seasonality → use multiplicative model

The births dataset shows fairly constant swings → additive expected.
The souvenir dataset shows swings that grow year on year → multiplicative expected.

3. Are there any cycles?
Multi-year business or economic cycles are visible as broad wave patterns that stretch beyond a single year. In the tourism employment example from the lecture slides, the 2008–2009 economic crisis created a visible dip and recovery cycle. Cycles are handled by the damped parameter in Holt methods.

2.4 Decomposition: Additive vs. Multiplicative

2.4.1 The Two Models

Additive (for constant seasonality): \[y_t = S_t + T_t + R_t\]

Multiplicative (for growing seasonality): \[y_t = S_t \times T_t \times R_t\]

You can convert multiplicative to additive using a log transformation: \[\log(y_t) = \log S_t + \log T_t + \log R_t\]

This is the Box-Cox transformation idea mentioned in class and in the Lecture 2 slides.

2.4.2 Running Decomposition in R

# Additive decomposition (default)
births.ts.comp <- decompose(births.ts)
plot(births.ts.comp)

# Multiplicative decomposition
souvenir.ts.comp <- decompose(souvenirtimeseries, type = "multiplicative")
plot(souvenir.ts.comp)

2.4.3 What to Look At in the Decomposition Output

The plot shows four panels: data → trend → seasonal → residual (random)

  • Trend panel: Should show smooth directional movement.
  • Seasonal panel: Should show a consistent, repeating pattern — the same shape each year. This is by definition: the seasonal component is the average of each period (mean of all Januaries, mean of all Februaries, etc.) repeated throughout.
  • Residual panel: This is the most important panel for model selection. You want it to look like white noise — no obvious patterns, no trend, no obvious clusters.

Key insight from class: If the residual panel of your additive decomposition looks like a shrunken version of the original data (you can still see the pattern), it means the model has not captured the structure properly. Switch to multiplicative.

2.4.4 Missing Values in Decomposition

The classical decompose() function uses a centred moving average to estimate the trend. Because this requires averaging neighbouring values symmetrically, it cannot produce estimates for the first and last \(p/2\) observations (where \(p\) is the frequency). For monthly data (\(p = 12\)), you lose 6 observations at each end of the trend and residual components.

2.5 Residual Analysis — Confirming Your Model

Once you have chosen a decomposition, extract the residuals and check four things:

residuals <- births.ts.comp$random
# Remove NAs introduced by moving average
residuals_clean <- na.omit(residuals)

1. Mean ≈ 0 and Mean ≈ Median (symmetry)

summary(residuals_clean)

If mean and median are close to zero and close to each other, the residuals are approximately symmetric.

2. Histogram — should look bell-shaped

hist(residuals_clean, main = "Residuals", xlab = "Residual value")

A good residual histogram is roughly symmetric, centred near zero.

3. Scatter plot / time plot — should look random

plot(residuals_clean, main = "Residuals over time")
abline(h = c(-2, 2), col = "blue")

About 95% of standardised values should fall within ±2 and 99% within ±4.

4. QQ plot — checks normality

qqnorm(residuals_clean)
qqline(residuals_clean, col = "red")

Points should sit close to the red diagonal line, especially in the central ±2 range. Small departures at the tails are acceptable.

Why does this matter? The residual distribution is what generates your confidence intervals. If your residuals follow a normal distribution centred on zero, you can construct statistically valid prediction intervals. If they don’t, your confidence intervals are unreliable.

2.6 STL Decomposition

STL = Seasonal and Trend decomposition using Loess (locally weighted regression)

Instead of calculating a simple moving average for the trend, STL fits a local regression line through small windows of data, sliding that window across the entire series. This avoids missing values at the ends.

library(forecast)
fit <- stl(elecequip, s.window = 7)
autoplot(fit) + xlab("year")

2.6.1 Key Parameters

Parameter What it controls
s.window Controls variation in the seasonal component. Use "periodic" to fix the season as constant.
t.window Controls smoothness of the trend. Larger = smoother trend.
robust = TRUE Makes estimation resistant to outliers.

2.6.2 Choosing the Right s.window

  • Too small (e.g. 5): The seasonal component is allowed to change too much → it absorbs signal that should be in the trend or residual.
  • "periodic" (recommended default): Forces the seasonal component to repeat identically every year — matches our theoretical definition of seasonality.

As shown in the lecture slides, using s.window = "periodic" usually gives a cleaner, more constant seasonal component and a more random-looking remainder.

2.6.3 Advantages and Disadvantages of STL

Advantages:

  • Robust to outliers (when robust = TRUE)
  • No missing values at series ends
  • Can handle seasonal component that changes gradually over time
  • User can control how smooth the trend and seasonal extraction are

Disadvantages:

  • No calendar effect (cannot model Easter, public holidays that shift dates each year)
  • Only handles additive models natively (apply log() transform first for multiplicative data)
  • Only handles one seasonal period (use mstl() for multiple seasonalities)

2.6.4 Useful STL-related R Functions

seasonal(fit)     # Extract seasonal component
trendcycle(fit)   # Extract trend-cycle component
remainder(fit)    # Extract residual component
seasadj(fit)      # Seasonally adjusted series (= data minus seasonal)
mstl(x)           # Multiple STL — handles more than one seasonal period

2.6.5 Seasonal Adjustment

After decomposition, you can strip out the seasonal component to expose the underlying trend more clearly:

Additive: \(y_t - S_t = T_t + R_t\)

Multiplicative: \(y_t / S_t = T_t \times R_t\)

# Additive seasonal adjustment
births.ts.sadj <- births.ts - births.ts.comp$seasonal
plot(births.ts.sadj)

Important caveat (from slides): Seasonally adjusted series still contain trend AND random noise, so they can look jagged and create “false” ups and downs. The trend-cycle component alone is smoother and better for identifying inflection points.

2.7 Forecasting with Decomposition (Naïve Methods)

Before using formal models, you can produce simple forecasts directly from decomposition:

  • The seasonal component is forecast by repeating the pattern from the last year (naïve seasonal forecast).
  • The trend-cycle is forecast using a simple method like naïve (random walk) on the seasonally adjusted series.
  • The two are combined to produce the full forecast.
# STL + naïve forecast
fit <- stl(elecequip, t.window = 13, s.window = "periodic")
fit %>% seasadj() %>% naive() %>% autoplot()

# Or use the stlf() shortcut
elecequip %>% stlf(method = "naive") %>% autoplot()

The confidence intervals come from the residual distribution — another reason clean, normal residuals are important.

2.8 Simple Exponential Smoothing (SES)

2.8.1 When to Use It

Use SES when your data:

  • Has no trend (or trend is negligible)
  • Has no seasonality
  • Is a confirmed time series (equally spaced)

2.8.2 The Core Idea

SES computes a weighted average of all past observations, where weights decrease exponentially as you go further back in time. More recent observations get more weight.

2.8.3 Key Equations

Forecast equation: \[\hat{y}_{t+h|t} = \ell_t\]

Smoothing (level) equation: \[\ell_t = \alpha y_t + (1 - \alpha)\ell_{t-1}\]

By iterating, this expands to: \[\hat{y}_{T+1|T} = \sum_{j=0}^{T-1} \alpha(1-\alpha)^j y_{T-j} + (1-\alpha)^T \ell_0\]

2.8.4 The Smoothing Parameter α

\[0 \leq \alpha \leq 1\]

α value Meaning
Close to 1 Short memory — almost all weight on last observation (approaches random walk)
Close to 0 Long memory — weights spread far back in history (approaches overall mean)

Trick for interpretation: If R estimates α = 0.83 (as in the oil data example), the model has a very short memory — your prediction will be close to your last known value.

2.8.5 Optimization

α and the initial level \(\ell_0\) are chosen by minimising the Sum of Squared Errors (SSE):

\[\text{SSE} = \sum_{t=1}^{T}(y_t - \hat{y}_{t|t-1})^2 = \sum_{t=1}^{T} e_t^2\]

This is a non-linear optimization problem (unlike OLS regression), so R uses numerical methods.

2.8.6 R Code

library(forecast)

# Method 1: ses() directly
fc <- ses(oildata, h = 5)
autoplot(fc) + autolayer(fitted(fc), series = "Fitted")

# Method 2: ets() family function — equivalent for SES
ses_model <- ets(train.ts, model = "ANN", alpha = 0.2)
# ANN = Additive error, No trend, No seasonality

2.8.7 SES Prediction Behaviour

Because SES produces a flat forecast (same value for all h steps ahead), the point forecast never changes after the last observation. However, the confidence interval widens as h increases, because the uncertainty accumulates with each step forward.

2.9 Overview of All Classical Models

From the lecture, the full family is:

Components Present Model R Function
No trend, no seasonality Simple Exponential Smoothing (SES) ses() or ets(..., model="ANN")
Trend only (linear) Holt’s Linear Method holt() or ets(..., model="AAN")
Trend + cycle/damping Holt Damped holt(..., damped=TRUE)
Trend + seasonality (constant) Holt-Winters Additive hw(..., seasonal="additive")
Trend + seasonality (growing) Holt-Winters Multiplicative hw(..., seasonal="multiplicative")
Best of all the above ETS (automatic) ets()

ETS stands for Error, Trend, Seasonality. The ets() function automatically selects the best model by minimising AICc.

3 Key Equations / Formulas

3.0.1 Time Series Decomposition

\[y_t = S_t + T_t + R_t \quad \text{(Additive)}\]

\[y_t = S_t \times T_t \times R_t \quad \text{(Multiplicative)}\]

3.0.2 Simple Moving Average (used internally in decompose())

\[F_t = MA_p = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{p} D_i}{p}\]

Where \(F_t\) = prediction, \(D_i\) = observation, \(p\) = periodicity (frequency)

3.0.3 SES Prediction Equation (expanded)

\[\hat{y}_{T+1|T} = \alpha y_T + \alpha(1-\alpha)y_{T-1} + \alpha(1-\alpha)^2 y_{T-2} + \cdots \quad 0 \leq \alpha \leq 1\]

3.0.4 SSE Optimization

\[\text{SSE} = \sum_{t=1}^{T}(y_t - \hat{y}_{t|t-1})^2\]

3.0.5 Prediction Error Metrics (mentioned, to be covered next class)

\[\text{MAD} = \frac{\sum |Y_t - F_t|}{n}\]

\[\text{MSE} = \frac{\sum (Y_t - F_t)^2}{n-1}\]

\[\text{MAPE} = \frac{1}{n} \sum \left|\frac{Y_t - F_t}{Y_t}\right| \times 100\]

4 Practical Examples from Class

4.0.1 Example 1 — Births Dataset (Additive)

  • Visual check: Increasing trend, constant seasonal amplitude → expect additive
  • Decompose: Both additive and multiplicative residuals look random, but additive is simpler → choose additive
  • Residual check: Mean ≈ Median ≈ 0, histogram is roughly normal, scatter plot is noisy within ±2 → decomposition confirmed as valid

4.0.2 Example 2 — Souvenir Dataset (Multiplicative)

  • Visual check: Increasing trend, growing seasonal amplitude → expect multiplicative
  • Decompose additive: Residual panel shows the same trend/seasonal pattern as the original data → additive decomposition is bad
  • Decompose multiplicative: Residual panel looks much more random → multiplicative confirmed
  • Residual check for additive: Mean ≈ −270, Median ≈ 98 (very different), histogram skewed → model inadequate
  • Residual check for multiplicative: Mean ≈ Median, histogram roughly symmetric, QQ plot shows minor tail departures only → model is valid

4.0.3 Example 3 — Oil Data (SES)

  • Data shows no clear trend or seasonality → SES appropriate
  • R estimates α = 0.8339 → short memory data
  • Point forecast is flat (constant); confidence intervals widen as horizon grows
  • AIC = 178.14, AICc = 179.86, BIC = 180.81 (used for model comparison — to be discussed next class)

5 Summary Points

  • Always verify you have a time series before analysis — check for equally spaced observations.
  • Always plot first to visually identify trend, seasonality type (additive/multiplicative), and potential cycles.
  • Decompose your data using decompose() or stl() and examine the residual panel — this is your primary diagnostic tool for model selection.
  • Prefer additive over multiplicative when both seem to work equally well (simpler is better).
  • Use log transformation to convert a multiplicative series to additive before applying STL.
  • Residuals should look normal (mean ≈ 0, symmetric, within ±2 for 95% of values) — this is what enables valid confidence intervals.
  • STL is more robust than decompose() — no missing values, less sensitive to outliers, controllable windows.
  • SES is for data with no trend and no seasonality. α near 1 = short memory (like random walk). α near 0 = long memory (like mean).
  • ETS is the unified family covering SES (ANN), Holt (AAN), and Holt-Winters (AAA or MAM etc.).
  • The classical models family progresses: SES → Holt → Holt-Winters, adding components (trend, then seasonality) as needed.

6 What Was Covered vs. What Is Still Pending

6.1 ✅ Covered in Week 4

Topic Source
Lab 1 walkthrough (kings, births, souvenir) Aula_4, Lab_1_SOL.R, Lab_1 handout
Identifying valid time series Aula_4
Visual component identification Aula_4 + Lecture 2 slides pp. 3–4
decompose() — additive vs. multiplicative Aula_4 + Lecture 2 p. 4, Lab 1 handout p. 2
Residual analysis (histogram, boxplot, QQ, scatter) Aula_4
STL decomposition overview Aula_4 + Lecture 2 slides pp. 20–24
Seasonal adjustment Aula_4 + Lecture 2 slides pp. 8–9
Forecasting with decomposition Aula_4 + Lecture 2 slides pp. 25–28
Simple Exponential Smoothing (SES) Aula_4 + Lecture 2 slides pp. 30–34

6.2 ⚠️ Started but NOT Finished

Topic Where to Check
Holt Linear Trend method Lecture 2 slides pp. 38–40 — READ THESE

Prediction error metrics (MAD, MSE, MAPE) Lecture 2 slides pp. 43–46 — READ THESE
Holt-Winters Additive Lecture 2 slides pp. 48 — READ THESE
Holt-Winters Multiplicative Lecture 2 slides pp. 49–53 — READ THESE
Holt-Winters Damped Lecture 2 slides pp. 54–55 — READ THESE
ETS in R (ets() function) Lecture 2 slides pp. 57–59 — READ THESE
AIC / BIC model selection Lecture 2 slides p. 36 + to be expanded next class

6.3 📋 Labs Assigned for Next Week

Francisco uploaded these to Canvas:

  1. Lab 1 Solution — the R script we went through today (already uploaded as Lab_1_SOL.R)
  2. Lab 2 — new lab on classical models; datasets include wine industry, air miles, unemployment rates, US accidents, stock market
  3. The unemployment rates dataset needs to be downloaded from Canvas (it is NOT built into R)

End of Week 4 Study Guide — STAT9005 Time Series

Source Code
---
title: "STAT9005 – Time Series: Week 4 Study Guide"
subtitle: "Classical Models – Lab Review, Decomposition & Exponential Smoothing"
---

> 📝 **Note to self:** These notes combine the Week 4 lecture (Aula_4), the Lab 1 handout, the Lab 1 solution R script, and the Lecture 2 Classical Models slides. Gaps and follow-up slides are flagged throughout.

---

# Key Topics

- Lab 1 walkthrough: Kings, Births, and Souvenirs datasets
- How to identify a valid time series vs. a non-time-series problem
- Visual identification of time series components (trend, seasonality, residual)
- Additive vs. multiplicative decomposition — choosing the right one
- Residual analysis: confirming a good decomposition
- STL decomposition (Seasonal & Trend decomposition using Loess)
- Forecasting using decomposition (naïve + STL-based)
- Introduction to Simple Exponential Smoothing (SES)
- Overview of the full classical models family (SES → Holt → Holt-Winters)
- Upcoming topics: Holt trend methods, Holt-Winters, error metrics, AIC/BIC

---

# Core Explanations

## Is It Actually a Time Series? — The Kings Dataset

Before doing any analysis, you must verify that your data actually qualifies as a time series. The first requirement is **equally spaced observations** — data points must be recorded at regular, consistent intervals.

The **Kings dataset** records the ages at death of successive Kings of England. At first glance it looks like sequential data, but it is *not* a time series. Each value is just the age a king happened to die — there is no regular time interval between observations. This is better framed as a **regression problem** (predicting the next king's age at death), not a forecasting problem. Francisco made this distinction explicitly in class: *we forget about the Kings dataset for this module.*

The rule of thumb: ask yourself *"Am I predicting future values of a process that unfolds over equally spaced time?"* If yes, it is a time series. If no, it is something else.

## Loading Data and the `ts()` Function

In R, raw data loaded with `scan()` is just a numeric vector — it has no time awareness. The `ts()` function wraps that vector with time series metadata.

```r
# Kings — no frequency info needed (annual-ish, frequency = 1 by default)
kings <- scan("http://robjhyndman.com/tsdldata/misc/kings.dat", skip = 3)
kingstimeseries <- ts(kings)

# Births — monthly data starting January 1946
births <- scan("http://robjhyndman.com/tsdldata/data/nybirths.dat")
birthstimeseries <- ts(births, frequency = 12, start = c(1946, 1))

# Souvenir sales — monthly data starting January 1987
souvenir <- scan("http://robjhyndman.com/tsdldata/data/fancy.dat")
souvenirtimeseries <- ts(souvenir, frequency = 12, start = c(1987, 1))
```

The key arguments are:

- `frequency` — number of observations per cycle (12 = monthly, 4 = quarterly, 1 = annual)
- `start` — vector giving the start time, e.g. `c(1946, 1)` means January 1946

Always plot immediately after creating the time series object to get your first visual read on the data.

```r
par(mfrow = c(1, 3))
plot.ts(kingstimeseries)
plot.ts(birthstimeseries)
plot.ts(souvenirtimeseries)
```

## Identifying Components Visually

Every time series is assumed to be a function of up to four components:

$$y_t = f(S_t, T_t, R_t)$$

Where:

- $y_t$ = observed value at time $t$
- $S_t$ = **seasonal component** — repeating patterns within a calendar year
- $T_t$ = **trend-cycle component** — long-run direction (includes multi-year cycles)
- $R_t$ = **remainder/residual** — everything unexplained (random noise)

When you plot your data, you are trying to answer three questions visually:

**1. Is there a trend?**  
Look for a consistent upward or downward direction over the full series. The births dataset shows a clear increasing linear trend, interrupted by a notable drop around 1955–1956.

**2. What type of seasonality is present?**  
Compare the *amplitude* (height) of seasonal swings across different years:
- If the swings stay roughly the same height → **homogeneous seasonality → use additive model**
- If the swings grow bigger as the series increases → **increasing/multiplicative seasonality → use multiplicative model**

The births dataset shows fairly constant swings → **additive expected**.  
The souvenir dataset shows swings that grow year on year → **multiplicative expected**.

**3. Are there any cycles?**  
Multi-year business or economic cycles are visible as broad wave patterns that stretch beyond a single year. In the tourism employment example from the lecture slides, the 2008–2009 economic crisis created a visible dip and recovery cycle. Cycles are handled by the **damped** parameter in Holt methods.

## Decomposition: Additive vs. Multiplicative

### The Two Models

**Additive** (for constant seasonality):
$$y_t = S_t + T_t + R_t$$

**Multiplicative** (for growing seasonality):
$$y_t = S_t \times T_t \times R_t$$

You can convert multiplicative to additive using a log transformation:
$$\log(y_t) = \log S_t + \log T_t + \log R_t$$

This is the **Box-Cox transformation** idea mentioned in class and in the Lecture 2 slides.

### Running Decomposition in R

```r
# Additive decomposition (default)
births.ts.comp <- decompose(births.ts)
plot(births.ts.comp)

# Multiplicative decomposition
souvenir.ts.comp <- decompose(souvenirtimeseries, type = "multiplicative")
plot(souvenir.ts.comp)
```

### What to Look At in the Decomposition Output

The plot shows four panels: **data → trend → seasonal → residual (random)**

- **Trend panel**: Should show smooth directional movement.
- **Seasonal panel**: Should show a *consistent, repeating* pattern — the same shape each year. This is by definition: the seasonal component is the average of each period (mean of all Januaries, mean of all Februaries, etc.) repeated throughout.
- **Residual panel**: ***This is the most important panel for model selection.*** You want it to look like white noise — no obvious patterns, no trend, no obvious clusters.

> **Key insight from class:** If the residual panel of your additive decomposition looks like a shrunken version of the original data (you can still see the pattern), it means the model has not captured the structure properly. Switch to multiplicative.

### Missing Values in Decomposition

The classical `decompose()` function uses a **centred moving average** to estimate the trend. Because this requires averaging neighbouring values symmetrically, it cannot produce estimates for the first and last $p/2$ observations (where $p$ is the frequency). For monthly data ($p = 12$), you lose 6 observations at each end of the trend and residual components.

## Residual Analysis — Confirming Your Model

Once you have chosen a decomposition, extract the residuals and check four things:

```r
residuals <- births.ts.comp$random
# Remove NAs introduced by moving average
residuals_clean <- na.omit(residuals)
```

**1. Mean ≈ 0 and Mean ≈ Median (symmetry)**
```r
summary(residuals_clean)
```
If mean and median are close to zero and close to each other, the residuals are approximately symmetric.

**2. Histogram — should look bell-shaped**
```r
hist(residuals_clean, main = "Residuals", xlab = "Residual value")
```
A good residual histogram is roughly symmetric, centred near zero.

**3. Scatter plot / time plot — should look random**
```r
plot(residuals_clean, main = "Residuals over time")
abline(h = c(-2, 2), col = "blue")
```
About 95% of standardised values should fall within ±2 and 99% within ±4.

**4. QQ plot — checks normality**
```r
qqnorm(residuals_clean)
qqline(residuals_clean, col = "red")
```
Points should sit close to the red diagonal line, especially in the central ±2 range. Small departures at the tails are acceptable.

> **Why does this matter?** The residual distribution is what generates your **confidence intervals**. If your residuals follow a normal distribution centred on zero, you can construct statistically valid prediction intervals. If they don't, your confidence intervals are unreliable.

## STL Decomposition

**STL** = **S**easonal and **T**rend decomposition using **L**oess (locally weighted regression)

Instead of calculating a simple moving average for the trend, STL fits a local regression line through small windows of data, sliding that window across the entire series. This avoids missing values at the ends.

```r
library(forecast)
fit <- stl(elecequip, s.window = 7)
autoplot(fit) + xlab("year")
```

### Key Parameters

| Parameter | What it controls |
|-----------|-----------------|
| `s.window` | Controls variation in the seasonal component. Use `"periodic"` to fix the season as constant. |
| `t.window` | Controls smoothness of the trend. Larger = smoother trend. |
| `robust = TRUE` | Makes estimation resistant to outliers. |

### Choosing the Right `s.window`

- **Too small** (e.g. 5): The seasonal component is allowed to change too much → it absorbs signal that should be in the trend or residual.
- **`"periodic"`** (recommended default): Forces the seasonal component to repeat identically every year — matches our theoretical definition of seasonality.

As shown in the lecture slides, using `s.window = "periodic"` usually gives a cleaner, more constant seasonal component and a more random-looking remainder.

### Advantages and Disadvantages of STL

**Advantages:**

- Robust to outliers (when `robust = TRUE`)
- No missing values at series ends
- Can handle seasonal component that changes gradually over time
- User can control how smooth the trend and seasonal extraction are

**Disadvantages:**

- No calendar effect (cannot model Easter, public holidays that shift dates each year)
- Only handles **additive** models natively (apply `log()` transform first for multiplicative data)
- Only handles **one** seasonal period (use `mstl()` for multiple seasonalities)

### Useful STL-related R Functions

```r
seasonal(fit)     # Extract seasonal component
trendcycle(fit)   # Extract trend-cycle component
remainder(fit)    # Extract residual component
seasadj(fit)      # Seasonally adjusted series (= data minus seasonal)
mstl(x)           # Multiple STL — handles more than one seasonal period
```

### Seasonal Adjustment

After decomposition, you can strip out the seasonal component to expose the underlying trend more clearly:

**Additive:** $y_t - S_t = T_t + R_t$

**Multiplicative:** $y_t / S_t = T_t \times R_t$

```r
# Additive seasonal adjustment
births.ts.sadj <- births.ts - births.ts.comp$seasonal
plot(births.ts.sadj)
```

> **Important caveat (from slides):** Seasonally adjusted series still contain trend AND random noise, so they can look jagged and create "false" ups and downs. The trend-cycle component alone is smoother and better for identifying inflection points.

## Forecasting with Decomposition (Naïve Methods)

Before using formal models, you can produce simple forecasts directly from decomposition:

- The **seasonal component** is forecast by repeating the pattern from the last year (naïve seasonal forecast).
- The **trend-cycle** is forecast using a simple method like naïve (random walk) on the seasonally adjusted series.
- The two are **combined** to produce the full forecast.

```r
# STL + naïve forecast
fit <- stl(elecequip, t.window = 13, s.window = "periodic")
fit %>% seasadj() %>% naive() %>% autoplot()

# Or use the stlf() shortcut
elecequip %>% stlf(method = "naive") %>% autoplot()
```

The confidence intervals come from the residual distribution — another reason clean, normal residuals are important.

## Simple Exponential Smoothing (SES)

### When to Use It

Use SES when your data:

- **Has no trend** (or trend is negligible)
- **Has no seasonality**
- Is a confirmed time series (equally spaced)

### The Core Idea

SES computes a **weighted average of all past observations**, where weights decrease exponentially as you go further back in time. More recent observations get more weight.

### Key Equations

**Forecast equation:**
$$\hat{y}_{t+h|t} = \ell_t$$

**Smoothing (level) equation:**
$$\ell_t = \alpha y_t + (1 - \alpha)\ell_{t-1}$$

By iterating, this expands to:
$$\hat{y}_{T+1|T} = \sum_{j=0}^{T-1} \alpha(1-\alpha)^j y_{T-j} + (1-\alpha)^T \ell_0$$

### The Smoothing Parameter α

$$0 \leq \alpha \leq 1$$

| α value | Meaning |
|---------|---------|
| Close to **1** | Short memory — almost all weight on last observation (approaches random walk) |
| Close to **0** | Long memory — weights spread far back in history (approaches overall mean) |

> **Trick for interpretation:** If R estimates α = 0.83 (as in the oil data example), the model has a very short memory — your prediction will be close to your last known value.

### Optimization

α and the initial level $\ell_0$ are chosen by minimising the **Sum of Squared Errors (SSE)**:

$$\text{SSE} = \sum_{t=1}^{T}(y_t - \hat{y}_{t|t-1})^2 = \sum_{t=1}^{T} e_t^2$$

This is a **non-linear optimization** problem (unlike OLS regression), so R uses numerical methods.

### R Code

```r
library(forecast)

# Method 1: ses() directly
fc <- ses(oildata, h = 5)
autoplot(fc) + autolayer(fitted(fc), series = "Fitted")

# Method 2: ets() family function — equivalent for SES
ses_model <- ets(train.ts, model = "ANN", alpha = 0.2)
# ANN = Additive error, No trend, No seasonality
```

### SES Prediction Behaviour

Because SES produces a **flat forecast** (same value for all h steps ahead), the **point forecast never changes** after the last observation. However, the **confidence interval widens** as h increases, because the uncertainty accumulates with each step forward.

## Overview of All Classical Models

From the lecture, the full family is:

| Components Present | Model | R Function |
|---|---|---|
| No trend, no seasonality | **Simple Exponential Smoothing (SES)** | `ses()` or `ets(..., model="ANN")` |
| Trend only (linear) | **Holt's Linear Method** | `holt()` or `ets(..., model="AAN")` |
| Trend + cycle/damping | **Holt Damped** | `holt(..., damped=TRUE)` |
| Trend + seasonality (constant) | **Holt-Winters Additive** | `hw(..., seasonal="additive")` |
| Trend + seasonality (growing) | **Holt-Winters Multiplicative** | `hw(..., seasonal="multiplicative")` |
| Best of all the above | **ETS (automatic)** | `ets()` |

**ETS** stands for **Error, Trend, Seasonality**. The `ets()` function automatically selects the best model by minimising AICc.

---

# Key Equations / Formulas

### Time Series Decomposition

$$y_t = S_t + T_t + R_t \quad \text{(Additive)}$$

$$y_t = S_t \times T_t \times R_t \quad \text{(Multiplicative)}$$

### Simple Moving Average (used internally in `decompose()`)

$$F_t = MA_p = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{p} D_i}{p}$$

Where $F_t$ = prediction, $D_i$ = observation, $p$ = periodicity (frequency)

### SES Prediction Equation (expanded)

$$\hat{y}_{T+1|T} = \alpha y_T + \alpha(1-\alpha)y_{T-1} + \alpha(1-\alpha)^2 y_{T-2} + \cdots \quad 0 \leq \alpha \leq 1$$

### SSE Optimization

$$\text{SSE} = \sum_{t=1}^{T}(y_t - \hat{y}_{t|t-1})^2$$

### Prediction Error Metrics (mentioned, to be covered next class)

$$\text{MAD} = \frac{\sum |Y_t - F_t|}{n}$$

$$\text{MSE} = \frac{\sum (Y_t - F_t)^2}{n-1}$$

$$\text{MAPE} = \frac{1}{n} \sum \left|\frac{Y_t - F_t}{Y_t}\right| \times 100$$

---

# Practical Examples from Class

### Example 1 — Births Dataset (Additive)

- **Visual check**: Increasing trend, constant seasonal amplitude → expect additive
- **Decompose**: Both additive and multiplicative residuals look random, but additive is simpler → **choose additive**
- **Residual check**: Mean ≈ Median ≈ 0, histogram is roughly normal, scatter plot is noisy within ±2 → **decomposition confirmed as valid**

### Example 2 — Souvenir Dataset (Multiplicative)

- **Visual check**: Increasing trend, *growing* seasonal amplitude → expect multiplicative
- **Decompose additive**: Residual panel shows the same trend/seasonal pattern as the original data → **additive decomposition is bad**
- **Decompose multiplicative**: Residual panel looks much more random → **multiplicative confirmed**
- **Residual check for additive**: Mean ≈ −270, Median ≈ 98 (very different), histogram skewed → model inadequate
- **Residual check for multiplicative**: Mean ≈ Median, histogram roughly symmetric, QQ plot shows minor tail departures only → **model is valid**

### Example 3 — Oil Data (SES)

- Data shows no clear trend or seasonality → SES appropriate
- R estimates α = 0.8339 → short memory data
- Point forecast is flat (constant); confidence intervals widen as horizon grows
- AIC = 178.14, AICc = 179.86, BIC = 180.81 (used for model comparison — to be discussed next class)

---

# Summary Points

- **Always verify** you have a time series before analysis — check for equally spaced observations.
- **Always plot first** to visually identify trend, seasonality type (additive/multiplicative), and potential cycles.
- **Decompose** your data using `decompose()` or `stl()` and **examine the residual panel** — this is your primary diagnostic tool for model selection.
- **Prefer additive** over multiplicative when both seem to work equally well (simpler is better).
- **Use log transformation** to convert a multiplicative series to additive before applying STL.
- **Residuals should look normal** (mean ≈ 0, symmetric, within ±2 for 95% of values) — this is what enables valid confidence intervals.
- **STL** is more robust than `decompose()` — no missing values, less sensitive to outliers, controllable windows.
- **SES** is for data with *no trend and no seasonality*. α near 1 = short memory (like random walk). α near 0 = long memory (like mean).
- **ETS** is the unified family covering SES (ANN), Holt (AAN), and Holt-Winters (AAA or MAM etc.).
- The **classical models family** progresses: SES → Holt → Holt-Winters, adding components (trend, then seasonality) as needed.

---
